content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
June 9,
1992
Deborah Cervone
Deputy City Clerk
City of Encinitas
527 Encinitas Blvd.
Encinitas,
CA
92024
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A-92-321
Dear Ms.
Cervone:
This is in response to your letter requesting advice
regarding the conflict—of—interest provisions of the Political
Reform Act
(the “Act”) .
You confirmed in our telephone
conversation of June 1,
1992, that you are requesting advice on
behalf of Deputy Mayor John Davis.
Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed
to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place.
In
addition, this letter is based on the facts presented to us.
The
Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice.
(In re Ogelsby
(1975)
1
FPPC Ops.
71.)
QUESTION
May Deputy Mayor Davis participate in an Encinitas City
Council decision regarding the removal of a traffic barrier which
may result in decreased traffic on
a street that runs within
300
feet of the deputy mayor’s residence?
CONCLUSION
The deputy mayor’s property will not result in a conflict of
interest with respect to the traffic barrier decision so long as
1
Government Code Sections 81000—91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 •
(916)322-5660


Our File No.
A—92—321
Page
2
the decision will not increase or decrease the fair market value
of his property by $10,000 or more.
PACTS
The Encinitas City Council is considering the removal of a
traffic barrier on Crest Drive.
The traffic barrier stopped
traffic on Crest Drive from traveling onto Santa Fe Street.
The
alternate route for traffic to reach Santa Fe Street is by means
of Lake Street.
Residents on Lake Street have asked that the
barrier on Crest Drive be removed to lessen traffic on their
street.
Deputy Mayor Davis’ residence is in a cul-de-sac on Lahound
Road.
His residence is
100 feet from Lake Street,
and
approximately one to one and one-fourth mile from the traffic
barrier.
ANALYSIS
You have asked whether the deputy mayor’s economic interest
in his personal residence will result in a conflict of interest
with respect to the traffic barrier decision.
The deputy mayor’s
interest in his real property is presumably worth more than
$1,000.
consequently,
he may not participate in any decision
which will have a foreseeable arid material financial effect on his
property which is distinguishable from the effect on the public
generally.
(Section 87100; Section 87103(b).)
The effects of a decision are reasonably foreseeable if tner€
is a substantial likelihood that they will occur.
To be
foreseeable, the effects of a decision must be more than a mere
possibility; however, certainty is not required.
(Downey Cares v.
Dowriey Development Corn.
(1987)
196 Cal.App.3d 983,
989—991; In re
Thorner
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
198.>
It is foreseeable that a
decision which will affect traffic flow on streets within 100 feet
of the deputy mayor’s residence will have some financial effect on
the value of the deputy mayor’s property.
However, the foreseeable financial effect on his property
must also be material to require disqualification.
You stated
that the site of the traffic barrier is approximately one to one
and one—fourth miles from the deputy mayor’s residence.
2
Regulation 18702.3(b) provides:
(b)
The reasonably foreseeable effect of a
decision is not considered material as to real
property in which an official has a direct,
2
As we discussed on our telephone conversation of June 1,
1992,
in measuring the distance between the property subject to the
decision and the deputy mayor’s property you would measure the
direct distance between the two closest points.


Our File No.
A—92—321
Page
3
indirect or beneficial interest
(not including a
leasehold interest), if the real property in which
the official has an interest is located entirely
beyond a 2,500 foot radius of the boundaries
(or
the proposed boundaries)
of the property which is
the subject of the decision; unless:
(1)
There are specific circumstances
regarding the decision, its effect,
and the
nature of the real property in which the
official has an interest, which make it
reasonably foreseeable that the fair market
value or the rental value of the real property
in which the official has an interest will be
affected by the amounts set forth in
subdivisions
(a) (3) (A)
or
(a) (3) (B) ; [3)
and
(2)
Either of the following apply:
(A)
The effect will not be
substantially the same as the effect upon
at least 25 percent of all the properties
which are within a 2,500 foot radius of
the boundaries of the real property in
which the official has an interest; or
(B)
There are not at least
10
properties under separate ownership
within a 2,500 foot radius of the
property in which the official has an
interest.
While your facts indicate that the traffic barrier is more
than 2,500 feet away from the deputy mayor’s property, it appears
likely,
if riot certain, that the decision on the barrier will have
some effect on the traffic flow near his property and could
therefore affect the property’s value.
However, it is not
apparent that the effect on traffic will increase or decrease the
fair market value of the deputy mayor’s real property by $10,000
or more.
We cannot make the factual determination of whether the
financial effect of the decision will reach the thresholds set
forth above.
Therefore,
we must leave this factual determination
Regulation 18702.3(a) (3)
provides that the effect on an
official’s real property is material if the decision will have a
reasonably foreseeable financial effect of:
(A)
$10,000 or more
on the fair market value of the real property in which the
official has an interest;
or
(B)
$1,000 or more on the rental
value of the property in a
12 month period.


Our File No.
A—92—321
Page
4
of materiality to the deputy mayor based upon the guidelines
provided in Regulation 18702.3.
If the decision will not increase
or decrease the fair market value of his property by $10,000 or
more, the deputy mayor may participate.
Regulation 18702.3(d) sets forth factors which must be
weighed in determining whether the decision will have a material
financial effect on the value of the deputy mayor’s real property.
The following must be considered:
1.
The proximity of the property which is the
subject of the decision and the magnitude of the
proposed project or change in use in relationship
to the property in which the official has an
interest;
2.
Whether it is reasonably foreseeable that
the decision will affect the development potential
or income producing potential of the property;
3.
In addition to the foregoing,
in the case
of residential property, whether it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will result in a
change to the character of the neighborhood
including, but not limited to, the effect on
traffic,
view, privacy, intensity of use, noise
levels, air emissions,
or similar traits of the
neighborhood.
Regulation 18702.3(d)
However,
even if the deputy mayor’s property will be affected
by $10,000 or more,
the deputy mayor may still participate in the
decision if the effect of the decision on the deputy mayor’s real
property will be substantially the same as the effect upon at
least 25 percent of all the properties which are within a 2,500
foot radius of his property,
and at least
10 of those properties
are under separate ownership.
(Regulation 18702.3(b) (2).)
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322-5901.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
ing General Counsel
Wallace
sel,
Legal Division
SH: JWW: aa
